event: Julius Caesar's legions conquer the region inhabited by the Belgic Treveri tribe, incorporating present-day Luxembourg into the Roman Empire.
Germanic Franks overrun the area as Roman authority collapses, beginning the Frankish settlement of the region.
The Treaty of Verdun divides Charlemagne's empire, placing the Luxembourg region within Lotharingia under Lothair I.
Count Siegfried acquires the rocky promontory and Roman fort of Lucilinburhuc, founding the dynasty and city that give Luxembourg its name.
Growth of the settlement and church around Siegfried's castle establishes Luxembourg as a fortified town.
Countess Ermesinde grants Luxembourg its town charter of freedoms, spurring urban development and trade.
Count Henry VII of Luxembourg is elected King of the Romans, later crowned Holy Roman Emperor in 1312.
John the Blind of Luxembourg, King of Bohemia, dies fighting at the Battle of Crecy and becomes a national legend.
Emperor Charles IV elevates the County of Luxembourg to a Duchy.
Philip the Good of Burgundy seizes Luxembourg, absorbing the duchy into the Burgundian state.
Through Habsburg-Burgundian marriage, Luxembourg passes to the House of Habsburg.
Luxembourg becomes part of the Spanish Netherlands following the division of the Habsburg empire under Philip II.
French forces under Louis XIV besiege and capture the fortress of Luxembourg, refortifying it under Vauban.
The Treaty of Ryswick returns Luxembourg to Spanish control after French occupation.
The Treaty of Rastatt transfers Luxembourg to the Austrian Habsburgs, beginning the Austrian Netherlands period.
Revolutionary France conquers and annexes Luxembourg, organizing it as the Departement des Forets.
The Congress of Vienna raises Luxembourg to a Grand Duchy in personal union with the King of the Netherlands and admits it to the German Confederation.
Most of Luxembourg joins the Belgian Revolution against Dutch rule, splitting the territory's allegiance.
The Treaty of London partitions Luxembourg, ceding the French-speaking western half to Belgium and confirming the remainder as an independent Grand Duchy.
Luxembourg joins the German Zollverein customs union, integrating its economy with the German states.
The opening of railway lines connects Luxembourg to neighboring markets and accelerates its industrialization.
The Second Treaty of London guarantees Luxembourg's perpetual neutrality and orders the dismantling of its fortress, ending the Luxembourg Crisis.
Luxembourg adopts a liberal constitution establishing parliamentary government under the Grand Duke.
The personal union with the Netherlands ends, and the Grand Duchy passes to the House of Nassau-Weilburg under Grand Duke Adolphe.
Founding of the ARBED steel company makes Luxembourg one of the world's leading steel producers.
Germany invades and occupies neutral Luxembourg at the outbreak of the First World War.
A referendum confirms the monarchy under Grand Duchess Charlotte and favors economic union with France.
Luxembourg enters the Belgium-Luxembourg Economic Union, linking its currency and trade policy to Belgium.
Nazi Germany invades and occupies Luxembourg, later annexing it and forcing its men into the Wehrmacht.
A general strike protests German conscription of Luxembourgers and is met with harsh Nazi reprisals.
Allied forces liberate Luxembourg, though it is again contested during the Battle of the Bulge.
Luxembourg becomes a founding member of the United Nations, abandoning its policy of neutrality.
Luxembourg joins the Benelux customs union with Belgium and the Netherlands and removes neutrality from its constitution.
Luxembourg becomes a founding member of the NATO alliance.
Luxembourg is a founding member of the European Coal and Steel Community, the precursor to the EU.
Luxembourg City becomes the provisional seat of the European Coal and Steel Community's High Authority.
Luxembourg signs the Treaty of Rome as a founding member of the European Economic Community.
Grand Duchess Charlotte abdicates in favor of her son, Grand Duke Jean.
The Schengen Agreement abolishing internal border controls is signed in the Luxembourg village of Schengen.
Luxembourg City is designated a European Capital of Culture, highlighting its cultural revival.
Luxembourg adopts the euro as a founding member of the eurozone.
Grand Duke Jean abdicates in favor of his son, Grand Duke Henri, the reigning monarch.
Luxembourgers approve the European Union constitutional treaty in a national referendum.
A constitutional reform removes the Grand Duke's power to sanction laws after Henri's refusal to sign a euthanasia bill.
Luxembourg launches a national initiative and later a law to support commercial asteroid and space mining ventures.
Luxembourg becomes the first country in the world to make all public transport free of charge.
Legislative elections bring a new coalition government, with Luc Frieden becoming Prime Minister.
